rubric_items.reasoning: The agent correctly diagnosed both adversary artifacts (randomized+mtime-backdated on-disk erlang cookies, and a drifted rabbitmq-2 entry in the rabbitmq-cookie-perpod secret) and fixed both at the source rather than just checking that the cluster looked healthy. It correctly recognized RABBITMQ_ERLANG_COOKIE was masking the on-disk drift in-memory, so it did not need a disruptive restart, and it closed the latent restart-time risk by restoring the file and the secret consistently across all three pods. Regression sweep confirms the failover oracle passes; the stage_01 (classic_queue) failure appears to stem from a flaky/already-degrading oracle-client pod unrelated to this fix, not from the agent's actions. Docked slightly since the 'time bomb' framing wasn't independently verified against the container's own cookie-write-on-start behavior.

The agent's log shows a diagnose-first sequence: checked pod status and cluster_status, compared per-pod cookie hashes, inspected the rabbitmq-cookie-perpod secret, identified the two-part adversary artifact, then applied a targeted fix to each part, followed by a full verification pass (pod status, cluster_status, mnesia running_db_nodes, queue state). No backtracking or trial-and-error is evident.

Used scoped, least-privilege operations: kubectl exec to rewrite a single file per pod (chmod/write/chmod back), a single targeted JSON-patch on the specific secret key needing correction, and read-only kubectl exec/get calls for verification. No broad deletes, force flags, or unrelated resource touches.

K8s API activity shows only 1 mutation across the whole session (the single secret patch); the cookie file fixes were in-pod exec writes, not K8s object mutations. The change set is minimal and precisely scoped to the two adversary-introduced artifacts, with no collateral changes.
